findings.description: The third column header in the HTML is labeled "Report # / Dissolved Units\Hg 1631" which incorrectly merges labels from different header rows. In the PDF, "Report #" and "Dissolved" are row labels for the sub-headers of the sampling-date columns (identifying which sample ID is the Report #, which is the Dissolved sample, and which is the Hg 1631 sample). They do not belong to the Units column. The column should be labeled "Units" or "Units\Hg 1631".
findings.evidence: PDF header rows stack: Row1 has 'Report #' above date columns; Row2 has 'Dissolved' above date columns; Row3 has 'Units\Hg 1631' for the units column. HTML merges all three into a single column header: "Report # / Dissolved Units\Hg 1631" for the column that only contains unit values like mg/L, ug/L, NTU.

findings.description: The PDF's multi-row header structure identifying three sample IDs per date (Report #, Lab/Dissolved, Hg 1631) is flattened into single-row headers in the HTML, and each date column is split into separate Value and Qualifier columns. While all data is present, the original header hierarchy and merged-cell structure is lost.
findings.evidence: PDF header for 2/14/2014 shows three stacked sample IDs: AC04986 (Report #), AC04987 (Lab/Dissolved), AC04993 (Hg 1631). HTML combines all into one header: '2/14/2014 AC04986 AC04987 AC04993 Value' plus a separate '2/14/2014 Qualifier' column.
